_meta.note: Terpene boiling points matter for vaporizer temperature. All concentrations are rough % of flower dry weight.

pharmacology.mechanisms: Increases blood-brain barrier permeability for cannabinoids; sedative/hypnotic via GABA modulation; muscle relaxant via spinal cord mechanism; anti-inflammatory via inhibition of prostaglandin E2; anti-mutagenic
pharmacology.evidence_quality: moderate (in vitro + animal; limited human RCT)

pain.note: Potentiates opioid and cannabinoid analgesia; reduces pain threshold in models

sleep.note: Primary sedative terpene in cannabis; induces couch-lock effect; indica high-myrcene strains classically sedating

muscle_spasm.note: Muscle relaxant in animal studies; relevant for MS spasticity, sports injury

inflammation.note: Inhibits PGE2 production; anti-mutagenic at high doses in cell models

anxiety.note: Sedation reduces anxiety indirectly; not primary anxiolytic mechanism

terpenes.vaporizer_temp_note: Vaporize at 175–185°C to capture myrcene alongside THC/CBD without combustion
terpenes.safety: Generally recognized as safe (GRAS in food); sedation at high concentrations; avoid driving

receptors: CB2 (selective full agonist — unique among terpenes)
pharmacology.mechanisms: Only terpene classified as a dietary cannabinoid; CB2-mediated immune modulation; potent anti-inflammatory via NF-κB suppression; reduces IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α; neuroprotective via CB2 in microglia; anti-tumor via PPARγ
pharmacology.evidence_quality: strong (CB2 agonism confirmed; multiple animal + some human trials)

pain.note: CB2-mediated pain relief without psychoactivity; particularly good for neuropathic and inflammatory pain (Bento et al. 2011)

inflammation.note: Reduces pro-inflammatory cytokines; IBD colitis models show significant mucosal protection

anxiety.note: Stress-anti-inflammatory; reduces anxiety-like behavior via CB2 in animal models (Bahi et al. 2014)

addiction.note: Reduces alcohol craving in animal models via CB2 pathway (Bahi et al. 2014)

neuroprotection.note: CB2 in microglia reduces neuroinflammation; relevant for PD, MS, Alzheimer's models

anti_tumor.note: PPARγ activation inhibits tumor growth in cell lines; humulene synergy in vitro

terpenes.vaporizer_temp_note: Boils at 160°C; present in most vaporizer sessions from 160°C upward
terpenes.safety: GRAS; no known adverse effects at normal consumption; may interact with blood thinners at very high doses (speculative)

pharmacology.mechanisms: Benzodiazepine-like sedation/anxiolytic WITHOUT direct BDZ binding; reduces cortisol; anti-convulsant; anti-nociceptive via adenosine A1; reduces neuroinflammation; pro-analgesic via spinal GABA
synergy_with: THC (anti-nociception, reduces anxiety side effects of THC)
pharmacology.evidence_quality: moderate (animal + limited human aromatherapy; mechanism well-characterized)

anxiety.note: GABA-A modulation; multiple human aromatherapy studies positive; reduces cortisol (Linck et al. 2009)

sleep.note: Lavender linalool extensively studied for sleep; cannabis linalool assumed similar (Salehi et al. 2019)

pain.note: Adenosine A1 anti-hyperalgesia; spinal GABAergic pain gate modulation (Peana et al. 2003)

ptsd.note: Reduces corticosterone stress response; reduces fear memory reconsolidation in animal models

epilepsy.note: Anticonvulsant in pentylenetetrazol models (Elisabetsky 1999); GABA mechanism

parkinsons.note: Reduces neuroinflammation; anti-Parkinsonian behavior in animal models; reduces tremor-anxiety cycle

terpenes.vaporizer_temp_note: High boiling point (198°C); requires higher vaporizer temperatures; most active via olfactory route in inhalation
terpenes.safety: Extensively studied; GRAS; may potentiate CNS depressants (sedatives, alcohol); not for first-trimester pregnancy

pharmacology.mechanisms: Mood-elevating via serotonin/dopamine; anti-anxiety (TRPC5 antagonism suppresses fear conditioned responses); anti-tumor via apoptosis in breast/prostate cancer cells; anti-reflux gastroprotective; immune stimulating (d-limonene)
pharmacology.evidence_quality: moderate (animal + small human trials for mood; stronger for gastric/anti-tumor)

anxiety.note: TRPC5 antagonism reduces fear response; 5-HT1A anxiolytic; but at high doses with high-THC can PARADOXICALLY worsen anxiety

depression.note: Increases 5-HT and dopamine; mood-elevating; Japanese aromatherapy studies (Komiya et al. 2006)

cancer.note: D-limonene Phase I human trials (Miller 2011): safe; anti-tumor biomarker changes in breast cancer

gerd.note: Anti-reflux; reduces esophageal acid exposure (Sun et al. 2007)

immune.note: Enhances immune function via NK cells (Raphael & Kuttan 2003)

terpenes.vaporizer_temp_note: 176°C; active in standard vaporizer range; dominant in many sativa strains
terpenes.caution_he: ריכוז גבוה עם THC גבוה עלול להגביר חרדה. עדיף בשילוב עם CBD. מתאים ביותר לשימוש בוקר עם CBD-dominant.

pharmacology.mechanisms: Counteracts THC short-term memory impairment (AChE inhibition); bronchodilator at low doses; anti-inflammatory (NF-κB); anti-microbial; alerting at high doses (antagonizes adenosine-induced sedation)
synergy_with: THC (memory protection against THC impairment — critical)
pharmacology.evidence_quality: moderate (human: bronchodilatory confirmed; AChE inhibition mechanism well-established)

memory_cognition.note: AChE inhibition: reduces THC-induced short-term memory impairment; potentially relevant for Alzheimer's adjunct

copd_asthma.note: Bronchodilator (Russo 2011); alpha-pinene studied for COPD; anti-inflammatory in airways

inflammation.note: NF-κB inhibition; anti-arthritis in animal models

alertness.note: Counteracts cannabis sedation; good for daytime use where alertness needed

anxiety.note: 5-HT1A mild agonism; but HIGH pinene may be alerting/stimulating — double-edged

terpenes.vaporizer_temp_note: Very low boiling point (156°C); vaporizes first in a session; set vaporizer to 160–165°C to capture specifically

receptors: Sedative (CNS mechanism unclear but consistent in animal models)
pharmacology.mechanisms: Potent sedative in animal models (Russo 2011 review); anti-oxidant; anti-proliferative in brain cancer cell lines (Aydin et al. 2013); anti-fungal
pharmacology.evidence_quality: limited (animal + in vitro; limited human data)

sleep.note: Highly sedative in mouse models; found in sleep-associated strains like OG Kush

cancer.note: Anti-proliferative in glioblastoma and neuroblastoma cell lines

terpenes.note: Often co-dominant with ocimene in certain sativa-leaning cultivars; strains with terpinolene as primary can be unpredictably sedating despite sativa morphology

pharmacology.mechanisms: Anti-inflammatory (COX inhibition similar to ibuprofen mechanism); anti-proliferative in tumor cells; appetite-suppressing (α-humulene isomer); synergizes with caryophyllene for superior anti-tumor effect
pharmacology.evidence_quality: moderate (animal + in vitro; synergy with caryophyllene strongest evidence)

inflammation.note: Anti-inflammatory comparable to NSAID mechanism in cell models (Fernandes et al. 2007)

cancer.note: Synergy with caryophyllene: superior cytotoxic effect vs each alone (Legault & Pichette 2007)

crohns_ibd.note: Potent gut anti-inflammatory; synergy with caryophyllene for IBD

appetite.note: α-humulene may be appetite-suppressing (could help obesity-IBD)

terpenes.vaporizer_temp_note: Very low boiling point (106°C) but survives longer in vapor; practical extraction at standard vaporizer temps

pharmacology.mechanisms: Anti-inflammatory; anti-viral (HSV inhibition); antidiabetic via aldose reductase + α-glucosidase inhibition; decongestant

antidiabetic.note: Enzyme inhibition in glucose metabolism pathways

pharmacology.mechanisms: Sedative; increases skin/membrane permeability (relevant for transdermal delivery); anti-malarial; anti-fungal

sleep.note: Secondary sedative terpene; found in indica-dominant sedating strains

topical_delivery.note: Skin penetration enhancer — relevant for topical cannabis products

pharmacology.mechanisms: Anti-inflammatory via COX inhibition; analgesic via TRPA1 modulation; skin penetration enhancer (improves transdermal bioavailability of other compounds); anti-irritant; anti-bacterial; promotes wound healing; mild anxiolytic via 5-HT1A
pharmacology.evidence_quality: moderate (in vitro + animal; human evidence from cosmetic/dermatology studies; limited cannabis-specific RCT)

skin_conditions.note: Widely used in dermatology; reduces skin irritation, promotes healing; anti-inflammatory in atopic dermatitis models (Maurya et al. 2019)

pain.note: TRPA1-mediated analgesic; anti-nociceptive in acute pain models; synergy with caryophyllene for neuropathic pain

anxiety.note: 5-HT1A partial agonism; mild anxiolytic; supports sleep onset via relaxing aroma

inflammation.note: COX inhibition; reduces pro-inflammatory cytokines; relevant for IBD topical formulations (Rocha et al. 2011)

neuroprotection.note: Reduces lipid peroxidation; antioxidant; potential Alzheimer's adjunct (Huang et al. 2017)

terpenes.vaporizer_temp_note: Low boiling point (153°C); vaporizes early in sessions alongside caryophyllene; most active transdermally
terpenes.safety: Extensively used in cosmetics (GRAS); no significant adverse effects at normal cannabis concentrations; pregnancy: insufficient data

pharmacology.mechanisms: Potent bronchodilator — clinically documented in asthma (Santos & Rao 2000); acetylcholinesterase inhibitor (memory enhancement; similar mechanism to donepezil); anti-inflammatory via 5-lipoxygenase inhibition (reduces LTB4, LTC4); anti-bacterial via membrane disruption; cognitive enhancement in aromatherapy trials
synergy_with: pinene (bronchodilatory + cognitive synergy — most studied pairing)
pharmacology.evidence_quality: strong for respiratory (multiple human RCTs); moderate for cognitive (human aromatherapy); moderate anti-inflammatory

copd_asthma.note: Reduces sputum viscosity; bronchodilator; anti-inflammatory in airways; approved in Germany as mucolytic (Soleimani et al. 2012); reduces corticosteroid requirement in asthma

memory_cognition.note: Inhalation in human trial: improved working memory and alertness (Moss et al. 2003; Tildesley et al. 2003 — rosemary 1,8-cineole)

inflammation.note: 5-LO pathway inhibition; reduces LTB4 (key inflammatory mediator in COPD, IBD, arthritis)

pain.note: Anti-nociceptive in animal models; synergy with pinene for neuropathic pain

antibacterial.note: Broad-spectrum antibacterial; relevant for oral/respiratory infections

terpenes.vaporizer_temp_note: Boils at 176°C; co-activates with limonene at standard vaporizer temperatures (175–185°C range)
terpenes.safety: GRAS; standard concentrations safe; high oral doses toxic (avoid ingestion of concentrated essential oil); inhalation at cannabis concentrations fully safe
terpenes.clinical_kill_switch_note: Patients with eucalyptol allergy (rare; cross-reactive with rosemary/eucalyptus sensitivity) should avoid high-cineole strains

receptors: GABA modulation (mild sedative in animal models)
pharmacology.mechanisms: Neuroprotective via reduction of oxidative stress in neuronal cells; anti-tumor via apoptosis induction (prostate, breast, colon cancer cell lines); anti-inflammatory via NF-κB and COX inhibition; TRPV1-mediated analgesia at low doses; anti-fungal; powerful insect repellent
pharmacology.evidence_quality: moderate (in vitro dominant; some animal studies; limited human data)

neuroprotection.note: Protects against glutamate-induced neuronal death; potential adjunct for Parkinson's and Alzheimer's (Rekha et al. 2013)

cancer.note: Induces apoptosis via caspase-3 in prostate, breast, pancreatic cancer cells in vitro; far from clinical application

pain.note: TRPV1 modulation reduces thermal hyperalgesia in models; anti-inflammatory analgesic (Rao et al. 2013)

anxiety.note: Rose-like aroma associated with anxiolytic response in olfactory studies; GABA modulation in animal models

antimicrobial.note: Inhibits Candida, Aspergillus, MRSA — relevant for immunocompromised patients (Rivas da Silva et al. 2012)

terpenes.vaporizer_temp_note: Very high boiling point (230°C); requires maximum vaporizer temperature settings; most effective via olfactory route or in combustion; largely missed by low-temp vaping
terpenes.safety: GRAS; skin sensitizer in some individuals (allergic contact dermatitis at high concentration in cosmetics); at cannabis concentrations, no clinical risk

terpene_interaction_matrix.note: Combinations with known synergistic effects based on pharmacological mechanism overlap

synergies.effect: Anti-inflammatory + analgesic synergy; IBD, chronic pain; both CB2 and prostaglandin pathways
Sedative amplification for sleep; both GABA-adjacent; best for PTSD nightmares, insomnia
Anti-tumor synergy (Legault 2007); anti-IBD; superior to either alone
Anxiolytic combination; serotonergic + GABAergic dual mechanism; anxiety + depression
Alerting + cognitive; counteracts THC sedation; daytime productivity; memory protection
Triple anti-inflammatory-analgesic-sedative; chronic pain at night; PTSD sleep formula
Skin + systemic anti-inflammatory dual pathway (COX + CB2); topical cannabis formulations; atopic dermatitis
Strongest documented respiratory synergy: bronchodilation + anti-inflammatory in airways; COPD, asthma, exercise-induced bronchospasm
Neuroprotection + anxiolytic; rose-lavender olfactory-CNS axis; Parkinson's adjunct + anxiety-driven insomnia
Deep sedation-relaxation-anti-irritant stack; fibromyalgia pain + sleep; post-surgery recovery
Anti-tumor preclinical trifecta; breast and prostate cell line apoptosis synergy; not yet clinical evidence
Respiratory + systemic anti-inflammatory; COPD exacerbations with comorbid pain; MS patients with respiratory weakness

clinical_kill_switch_triggers.note: Peer-reviewed evidence for terpene fractions that may worsen specific medical conditions at high concentrations. These inform the Clinical Kill-Switch engine in api/lib/clinicalCore.js. All thresholds are guidance-level — individual sensitivity varies.

triggers.mechanism: High alpha-pinene acts as a GABA-A *antagonist* (alerting/stimulating) at concentrations above ~15% of total terpene load. For PTSD patients, this can trigger hyperarousal, heightened sensory sensitivity, and panic episodes — the opposite of desired therapeutic effect. CB1 suppression of aversive memory extinction may also be partially blocked.
references: Satou et al. 2014: alpha-pinene inhalation increased locomotor activity and anxiety-like behavior in elevated plus maze at high dose
Russo 2011: notes pinene alerting vs. linalool/myrcene sedating polarity
triggers.clinical_action: Block strains where pinene fraction ≥ 15% of total terpene profile for patients with active PTSD or panic disorder indication

triggers.mechanism: Terpinolene, while sedating in rodent models, produces paradoxical stimulant-like effects in some human users, particularly when combined with high THC. Its mechanism of CNS effect is poorly characterized (no established receptor target). High-terpinolene strains are consistently reported by anxiety-prone patients as anxiogenic despite the sativa-leaning genetic profile.
references: Aydin et al. 2013: terpinolene CNS stimulant properties in HT-22 cells
Community-level pharmacovigilance: terpinolene-dominant strains (Dutch Treat, Ghost Train Haze) frequently reported as anxiety-triggering
triggers.clinical_action: Flag (not block) strains where terpinolene is dominant (>20% of terpene fraction) for anxiety-primary patients; recommend only with CBD co-administration

triggers.mechanism: Limonene is anxiolytic at moderate concentrations (5-HT1A agonism, TRPC5 antagonism) but becomes dose-dependently anxiogenic at high concentrations when combined with high THC. The stimulatory dopaminergic component can exacerbate THC-induced anxiety at D2 receptors. This is a THC×limonene interaction, not limonene alone.
triggers.applies_only_when: THC category ≥ T15/C3 AND limonene fraction > 30% of total terpene load
references: Berger et al. 2022 (Front Psychiatry): CBD-dominant reduced anxiety 58%; THC-dominant strains with high limonene had highest anxiety risk
Kaplan et al. 2017: THC increases anxiety dose-dependently; limonene does not mitigate at high THC doses
triggers.clinical_action: Block high-limonene (>30%) strains in T15+ categories for anxiety-primary patients; permissible in CBD-dominant formulations (T1-T3)

triggers.mechanism: Beta-caryophyllene at very high oral doses (pharmacological levels, not typical cannabis inhalation) may interact with warfarin metabolism via CYP2C9 modulation. At normal cannabis inhalation concentrations, clinical significance is likely low. Caution flag for patients on narrow therapeutic index anticoagulants.
triggers.applies_only_when: High-dose oral cannabis (oils/edibles) with caryophyllene fraction > 50% AND patient on warfarin/anticoagulant
triggers.evidence_strength: limited (speculative; in vitro CYP interaction; no human RCT)
references: Di Giacomo et al. 2020 (Molecules): caryophyllene CYP2C9 modulation in vitro
triggers.clinical_action: Advisory flag only for oral cannabis with very high caryophyllene in anticoagulated patients; not a hard block; recommend INR monitoring

triggers.mechanism: 1,8-Cineole inhibits CYP3A4 and CYP2B6 at high concentrations, which could increase plasma levels of clobazam and N-desmethylclobazam in patients with epilepsy already at risk of clobazam toxicity. The interaction is primarily relevant for high-dose oral eucalyptol; cannabis inhalation concentrations are likely sub-threshold. Flagging is precautionary.
triggers.applies_only_when: Patient on clobazam (Onfi) AND using high-dose oral cannabis oil with significant eucalyptol content
triggers.evidence_strength: limited (CYP modulation in vitro; interaction speculative at cannabis doses)
references: Ciriino et al. 2016: 1,8-cineole inhibits CYP3A4 in human liver microsomes
Gaston et al. 2017: clobazam-CBD interaction established — precautionary extension to eucalyptol
triggers.clinical_action: Advisory flag for epilepsy patients on clobazam using oral cannabis with detectable eucalyptol; recommend monitoring for clobazam side effects

160_165.effect: Anti-inflammatory, pain relief start; light vapor

175_185.effect: Optimal full-spectrum; anxiety relief + sedation begins

185_195.effect: Deeper sedation; good for sleep strains; more CBN

195_plus.warning: Combustion risk; degradation products; not recommended for therapeutic vaporization
